the ballerina performs 3 spins followed by 2 leaps, and this sequence repeats every 5 minutes. the total transformation matrix $ T $ for one complete sequence of 3 spins followed by 2 leaps can be derived by combining the rotation matrices for the spins and the vertical displacement vectors for the leaps. each spin is modeled by a rotation matrix $ R(\theta) $ with $ \theta = 2\pi $ radians. the matrix for a single spin is $ R(2\pi) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $, since a rotation of $ 2\pi $ radians is a full rotation and returns to the original position. the matrix for a single leap is $ \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $, since a leap is a vertical displacement vector. the total transformation matrix $ T $ for one complete sequence of 3 spins followed by 2 leaps is the product of the rotation matrices for the spins and the displacement vectors for the leaps. the matrix for 3 spins is $ R(2\pi)^3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $, and the matrix for 2 leaps is $ \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $. the total transformation matrix $ T $ is therefore $ \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $.

the ballerina earns $1.50 for each spin and $2.50 for each leap. the total earnings for a day can be calculated by multiplying the number of spins and leaps by their respective earnings. the ballerina performs 3 spins and 2 leaps every 5 minutes, and she busks for 3 hours each day. the total number of spins in a day is $ \frac{3 \text{ hours}}{5 \text{ minutes}} \times 3 \text{ spins} = 36 \text{ spins} $. the total number of leaps in a day is $ \frac{3 \text{ hours}}{5 \text{ minutes}} \times
